beats.prompt: Quick warm-up — tap to match each famous result to the formula that made it.

pairs.left: (3,4,3) — a drunkard's steps to a wall

feedback.correct: Each headline number is the fingerprint of one tool: a probability **split** with no +1 (absorption), a step-**count** with a +1 (hitting time), or a long-run **share** (stationary). The result reveals the question that was asked.
hints: Warm-up — match each famous result to the formula that produced it.
A probability split (no +1) is absorption; a step-count (+1 every move) is hitting time; a long-run fraction is the stationary share — the +1 is the only thing separating the first two.
4/7 → absorption (I−Q)⁻¹R · (3,4,3) → hitting time (I−Q)t=1 · (3/7,4/7) → stationary share πP=π.

beats.prompt: An unlabeled Ehrenfest urn: balls rattle between two boxes, bouncing forever. Before classifying — which tool does it call for?

options: Absorption — the balls eventually get stuck in one box

Absorption — the balls eventually get st.note: Bouncing forever is the *opposite* of stuck. The Ehrenfest urn has NO absorbing state — every count leads back out, so there is nothing to be absorbed into. Ask the long-run share, πP=π.

Stationary share (πP=π) — it mixes forev.note: Right — every state leads back out, so the chain never settles into one box. Ask what fraction of the time it spends at each count: (1/4, 1/2, 1/4).

Convergence (Pⁿ→π) — the distribution se.note: Careful — Ehrenfest is *periodic* (period 2): parity flips every step, so Pⁿ never settles. The long-run *time-average* (πP=π) still exists — that's the answerable question, not Pⁿ-convergence.

hints: No wrong guess yet — commit, and we'll test it by structure.
Balls that bounce forever are the opposite of stuck — there is no state they can't leave, so 'when/where absorbed' is the wrong question.
No absorbing state ⇒ ask the long-run share πP=π → (1/4, 1/2, 1/4).

beats.prompt: Expand the primer — read the one fork that decides the tool.

interaction.title: One fork decides the tool
interaction.body: Before computing anything, ask: **is there a state the chain can never leave?** **Yes → absorbing:** ask *which* exit (absorption probability (I−Q)⁻¹R) and *how long* until it's trapped (hitting time (I−Q)t=1). **No → it mixes forever (ergodic):** ask the *long-run share* (πP=π, Kac return time 1/πᵢ). Read the **structure, not the story** — and the tool picks itself.

feedback.correct: One fork, then the tool picks itself: can the chain reach a state it can never leave?
hints: Ask one structural question before any algebra.
A state you can't leave = absorbing → ask which / when. No such state = ergodic → ask the long-run share.
Absorbing → (I−Q)⁻¹R & (I−Q)t=1 · Ergodic → πP=π & 1/πᵢ.

beats.prompt: Is the weather chain absorbing or ergodic? Tap its class.

feedback.correct: Neither Clear nor Rainy is a trap — each leads back to the other, so the weather **mixes forever**. That's **ergodic** ⇒ ask the long-run share, πP=π = (3/7, 4/7).
hints: Look for a state with no way out. Is there one?
A 'bad-weather' state is not a trap — Rainy still leads back to Clear. No state is absorbing, so the chain never gets stuck.
No absorbing state ⇒ ergodic ⇒ tap 'ergodic → stationary share' (πP=π = 3/7, 4/7).

beats.prompt: Watch the absorbing drunkard slam into a wall — replay L5's tools.

hero.structuralReadout: Walls at 0 & 4 are absorbing — from the middle (start 2) the drunkard reaches a wall w.p. 1/2 each side; reach P = i/4 = (1/4, 1/2, 3/4), steps to a wall = i(4−i) = (3, 4, 3).

feedback.correct: The walls at 0 and 4 swallow the walker — once he hits one he never leaves. That's **absorbing**: ask *which* wall (reach P = i/4 = (1/4, 1/2, 3/4)) and *how long* (steps = i(4−i) = (3, 4, 3)).
hints: Watch where the walk ends — does it ever come back?
A symmetric walk has NO long-run share of the middle: the walls absorb it, so the chain ends rather than settling.
Walls are absorbing ⇒ reach P = i/4 = (1/4, 1/2, 3/4), steps = i(4−i) = (3, 4, 3).

beats.prompt: Watch the rubric at work: cycle through Ehrenfest, weather, and drunkard — classify each and see the right tool resolve.

hero.structuralReadout: no exit ⇒ πP=π · has an exit ⇒ (I−Q)⁻¹R / (I−Q)t=1

feedback.correct: Same fork, three costumes: weather and the Ehrenfest urn have **no exit** ⇒ stationary share (πP=π); the drunkard has **walls** ⇒ absorption (I−Q)⁻¹R / hitting time (I−Q)t=1. The structure, not the story, picks the tool.
hints: Classify each chain first: is there a state it can never leave?
The urn / weather / walk costumes don't matter — two mix forever (ergodic), one has walls (absorbing). Pick by structure.
Ergodic → πP=π (weather 3/7,4/7; Ehrenfest 1/4,1/2,1/4) · Absorbing → absorption (1/4,1/2,3/4) / time (3,4,3).

beats.prompt: Match each structural cue to the tool it triggers.

pairs.left: A state you can never leave (a wall)
How many steps until you hit that wall
Every state leads back out — it mixes forever
How often you return to a state

beats.interviewNote: The first move on *any* chain problem is to classify it — absorbing or ergodic — because that single fork decides whether you set up (I−Q)⁻¹ (which exit / how long) or πP=π (the long-run share). Naming the fork out loud is half the solution.

feedback.correct: Each structural **cue** names its tool: a state you can't leave → absorption; the steps to reach it → hitting time (the +1); every state leads back out → stationary share; how often you return → Kac time. Classify first, compute second.
hints: Match by structure: what does each cue tell you about exits?
Absorption probability and hitting time are NOT one computation — both use (I−Q)⁻¹, but hitting time adds +1 per step. 'Which end' vs 'how long' is the difference.
Can't-leave → absorption (I−Q)⁻¹R · steps-to-it → hitting time (I−Q)t=1 · leads-back-out → πP=π · how-often-return → 1/πᵢ.

beats.prompt: Decide the tool for each problem first — then compute.

interaction.scenario: Two unlabeled problems. **Part A:** a walker on \(\{0,1,2,3,4\}\) steps \(\pm 1\) with equal chance; 0 and 4 stop it — find the chance it reaches 4 starting from 1. **Part B:** a sky flips Clear\(\leftrightarrow\)Rainy forever by \(P=[[3/5,2/5],[3/10,7/10]]\) — find the long-run fraction of Clear days. Decide the tool for each *first*.

feedback.correct: Part A has **walls** (the drunkard at 0 and 4 is absorbing) \(\Rightarrow\) absorption: \(P(\text{reach 4} \mid \text{start 1}) = i/N = \mathbf{1/4}\). Part B **mixes forever** (the weather is ergodic) \(\Rightarrow\) stationary: long-run Clear share \(= \mathbf{3/7}\). The cover story is a costume — classify by structure.
hints: Don't compute yet — first ask each part: is there a state it can never leave?
Pick the tool by *structure*, not the cover story: Part A has absorbing walls (ask which / when); Part B mixes forever (ask the long-run share).
Part A absorption i/N = 1/4 · Part B stationary π_Clear = 3/7.

beats.prompt: Classify the chain and give the full absorption vector.

interaction.scenario: A frog sits on lily pads \(0\text{–}4\) and hops left / right with equal chance. Pads **0** and **4** are sink pads — land there and it stops forever. Classify the chain, then give the chance it ends on pad 4 from each interior start.

fields.label: Is this chain absorbing or ergodic?

feedback.correct: A frog on pads \(0\text{–}4\) with sink pads 0 & 4 is a **symmetric absorbing walk** — *not* ergodic, so 'long-run share' is the trap. \[\text{Absorption: } P(\text{reach pad 4} \mid \text{start } i) = i/4 \Rightarrow \mathbf{(1/4,\; 1/2,\; 3/4)}\]
hints: It runs a while — but does it ever leave pad 0 or pad 4? Classify before you compute.
'It runs a long time, so ask its long-run share' is the trap: the sink pads are absorbing, so the chain ends. Ask *which* pad and *how likely*, not a long-run fraction.
Absorbing ⇒ absorption vector i/4 = (1/4, 1/2, 3/4): start 1 → 1/4, start 2 → 1/2, start 3 → 3/4.

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap — one fork, three tools, exact rationals.

feedback.correct: Every chain question forks on one structural test — *is there a state the chain can never leave?* **Yes → absorbing** (which exit (I−Q)⁻¹R, how long (I−Q)t=1); **No → ergodic** (long-run share πP=π, return 1/πᵢ). Read the structure, not the story.
hints: Absorbing walls ⇒ which / when: gambler 4/7, drunkard (1/4,1/2,3/4) & (3,4,3).
Ergodic mixing ⇒ long-run share: weather (3/7,4/7), cloudy-town (1/5,2/5,2/5), Ehrenfest (1/4,1/2,1/4).
Periodic warning: Ehrenfest's πP=π share exists, but Pⁿ never settles (period 2). That's the whole concept in one fork — you're done.
